definition: Emitting or reflecting light; bright or shining
example_1: The luminous stars filled the night sky.
example_2: Her luminous smile lit up the room.

definition: Lasting for a very short time
example_1: The ephemeral beauty of cherry blossoms lasts only days.
example_2: Fame can be ephemeral in the age of social media.

definition: A powered railway vehicle used for pulling trains
example_1: The steam locomotive puffed clouds of white smoke.
example_2: The locomotive pulled twenty cars across the mountains.

definition: Relating to a transitional stage between two states
example_1: Dawn is a liminal time between night and day.
example_2: She stood in the liminal space between childhood and adulthood.

definition: A small waterfall; a series of stages or processes
etymology: French cascade, from Italian cascata (to fall)
example_1: Water tumbled down the cascade in sparkling sheets.
example_2: One mistake triggered a cascade of problems.

definition: Holding firm; persistent and determined
example_1: The tenacious climber reached the summit despite the storm.
example_2: Her tenacious spirit helped her overcome every obstacle.

definition: A picture or pattern produced by arranging small pieces of stone, tile, or glass
etymology: Medieval Latin mosaicus, from Greek mouseios (of the Muses)
example_1: The ancient mosaic depicted a Roman feast.
example_2: The city's culture is a mosaic of different traditions.

definition: Able to recover quickly from difficulties; flexible
example_1: Resilient communities rebuild stronger after disasters.
example_2: The resilient material returns to its shape after bending.

definition: Either of the two times in the year when the sun reaches its highest or lowest point
etymology: Latin solstitium, from sol (sun) + sistere (to stand still)
example_1: The winter solstice marks the shortest day of the year.
example_2: Ancient peoples celebrated the summer solstice with festivals.

definition: Showing luminous colors that seem to change when seen from different angles
example_1: The butterfly's iridescent wings shimmered in the sunlight.
example_2: The soap bubble was a perfect iridescent sphere.

definition: To continue in a course of action despite difficulty or delay
example_1: She persevered through years of training to become a doctor.
example_2: You must persevere even when the task feels impossible.

definition: A group of islands
etymology: Italian arcipelago, from Greek archi (chief) + pelagos (sea)
example_1: Indonesia is the world's largest archipelago.
example_2: The explorer sailed through the archipelago for three days.

definition: Green with grass or other rich vegetation
etymology: Old French verdant, from Latin viridis (green)
example_1: The verdant hills rolled down to the sea.
example_2: After the rain, the garden was verdant and alive.

definition: The quantity of motion; the impetus gained by movement
example_1: The boulder gained momentum as it rolled downhill.
example_2: The team's early win gave them momentum for the season.

definition: Playfully quaint or fanciful; acting on impulse
example_1: The whimsical illustrations delighted the children.
example_2: She made a whimsical decision to paint the fence purple.

definition: The line at which the earth's surface and the sky appear to meet
example_1: The ship disappeared over the horizon at sunset.
example_2: New opportunities lay just beyond the horizon.

definition: Fluent or persuasive in speaking or writing
etymology: Latin eloquens, from eloqui (to speak out)
example_1: The eloquent speaker moved the crowd to tears.
example_2: She wrote an eloquent letter defending her position.

definition: To understand after much thought; a unit of depth equal to six feet
example_1: Scientists could not fathom the depth of the trench.
example_2: She couldn't fathom why he'd made that choice.

definition: The occurrence of fortunate events by chance
etymology: From the fairy tale The Three Princes of Serendip
example_1: Meeting her future husband on a canceled flight was pure serendipity.
example_2: Scientific discoveries often rely on serendipity.

definition: Courage in pain or adversity
example_1: The soldiers showed great fortitude in battle.
example_2: It takes fortitude to start over after failure.

definition: A cloud of gas and dust in space, often where stars are born
example_1: The Orion Nebula glows with the light of newborn stars.
example_2: Astronomers study nebulae to understand how galaxies form.

definition: Skill in performing tasks with the hands; mental agility
example_1: A surgeon needs great dexterity.
example_2: The pianist's dexterity was astounding.

definition: Free from disturbance; calm and peaceful
example_1: The lake was tranquil at dawn.
example_2: She found tranquil moments reading in the garden.

definition: To move back and forth in a regular rhythm; to waver between opinions
example_1: The pendulum oscillated with perfect regularity.
example_2: She oscillated between excitement and anxiety about the move.

definition: A covering over a throne or bed; the uppermost layer of a forest
example_1: Sunlight filtered through the forest canopy.
example_2: The royal canopy shielded the king from the sun.

definition: An orange-red fruit with sweet flesh when fully ripe
example_1: The persimmon hung heavy on the autumn branches.
example_2: Ripe persimmons taste like honey with a floral note.

definition: A complicated irregular network of passages; a maze
example_1: The Minotaur lived at the center of the labyrinth.
example_2: The old city's streets formed a labyrinth of alleys.

definition: A gradual change in a value over a distance or area
example_1: The color gradient shifted from blue to violet.
example_2: The steep gradient made cycling uphill exhausting.

definition: The highest point reached; the point directly above an observer
etymology: Medieval Latin cenit, from Arabic samt (path)
example_1: The sun reached its zenith at noon.
example_2: Her career was at its zenith when she won the award.

definition: Producing strong, clear images in the mind; intensely bright
example_1: She had vivid memories of her grandmother's kitchen.
example_2: The painting's vivid colors demanded attention.

definition: A substance that speeds up a reaction; a person or thing that causes change
example_1: The catalyst reduced the reaction time by half.
example_2: Her speech was a catalyst for the protest movement.

definition: To match or surpass by imitation; to copy with improvement
example_1: Young artists often emulate the masters.
example_2: The new software emulates the behavior of older systems.

definition: The state of being alone, especially when peaceful
example_1: She valued solitude for deep thinking.
example_2: The cabin offered perfect solitude in the wilderness.

definition: A curve or figure where each part has the same pattern as the whole
etymology: Latin fractus (broken), coined by Benoit Mandelbrot
example_1: Snowflakes are natural fractals.
example_2: Fern leaves display beautiful fractal patterns.

definition: In its original condition; unspoiled; clean and fresh
example_1: The pristine beach had never been visited by tourists.
example_2: She kept her books in pristine condition.

definition: A general proposition proved on the basis of axioms
etymology: Greek theorema (spectacle), from theorein (to look at)
example_1: Pythagoras's theorem describes right triangles.
example_2: The mathematician spent years proving the theorem.

definition: A person who moves from place to place without a fixed home
example_1: Nomads followed the herds across the steppe.
example_2: As a digital nomad, she worked from a new country each month.

definition: The process of coming together; the point where things meet
example_1: The convergence of the rivers formed a delta.
example_2: A convergence of events led to the discovery.

definition: Filled with lively energy and excitement
example_1: The children gave an exuberant welcome to the puppy.
example_2: Her exuberant personality filled every room she entered.

definition: Relating to tides; rising and falling like tides
example_1: Tidal pools shelter unique ecosystems.
example_2: A tidal wave of protest swept the country.

definition: Relating to migration; moving seasonally between regions
example_1: Migratory birds travel thousands of miles each year.
example_2: The migratory route crosses three continents.

definition: The arrangement of words and phrases to create well-formed sentences
example_1: Good syntax makes writing clear and easy to follow.
example_2: The programmer found a syntax error in the code.

definition: Height above sea level; the angle of a celestial body above the horizon
example_1: Altitude sickness affects climbers above 8000 feet.
example_2: The plane cruised at an altitude of 35,000 feet.

definition: A statement that seems contradictory but may be true
example_1: The Ship of Theseus is a famous paradox.
example_2: It's a paradox that silence can be deafening.

definition: Having or showing intense feeling; passionate
example_1: She was a fervent supporter of the cause.
example_2: His fervent plea moved the judge.

definition: The blocking of light from one celestial body by another
example_1: The total solar eclipse turned day into darkness.
example_2: His early success was eclipsed by later achievements.

definition: To cause to occur at the same time or rate
example_1: The swimmers synchronized their movements perfectly.
example_2: She synchronized her watch with the official clock.

definition: The great size or extent of something; the degree of brightness of a star
example_1: The magnitude of the disaster was overwhelming.
example_2: Astronomers measure stellar magnitude on a numeric scale.

definition: The ability to understand something immediately without reasoning
etymology: Latin intuitio, from intueri (to look at)
example_1: Her intuition told her something was wrong.
example_2: Good chefs develop intuition about flavor combinations.

definition: Emitting light without combustion or perceptible heat
example_1: Phosphorescent plankton lit the waves blue.
example_2: The dial glowed with phosphorescent paint.

definition: The quality of being made up of exactly similar parts facing each other
example_1: The butterfly's wings showed perfect symmetry.
example_2: Architects use symmetry to create balance.

definition: To prepare land for crops; to develop a skill or relationship
example_1: Farmers cultivate the soil each spring.
example_2: She cultivated friendships with musicians and artists.

definition: A fact or situation that is observed to exist; a remarkable person or thing
etymology: Greek phainomenon (thing appearing), from phainein (to show)
example_1: The aurora borealis is a natural phenomenon.
example_2: She was a phenomenon — winning three championships before age 20.

definition: A structure of crossed strips with gaps between; a framework
example_1: Roses climbed the garden lattice.
example_2: Crystal atoms arrange in a regular lattice.

definition: Great energy or enthusiasm in pursuit of a cause
example_1: She approached every project with infectious zeal.
example_2: His zeal for justice drove his entire career.

definition: Natural light display in the sky, especially near polar regions
etymology: Latin aurora (dawn), Roman goddess of dawn
example_1: The aurora borealis painted the sky in green and pink.
example_2: They traveled to Iceland to see the aurora.

definition: To imitate the appearance or function of something
example_1: The software simulates weather patterns.
example_2: Pilots train in devices that simulate cockpit conditions.

definition: In a sleeping or inactive state; temporarily inactive
example_1: The dormant volcano last erupted 200 years ago.
example_2: The seeds remained dormant until spring.

definition: Showing great attention to detail; very careful and precise
example_1: The meticulous surgeon left perfect stitches.
example_2: She kept meticulous records of every expense.

definition: The quality of being resonant; an effect that endures or strengthens
example_1: The bell's resonance filled the valley.
example_2: The speech had an emotional resonance that lasted for years.

definition: A river or stream flowing into a larger river
example_1: The Missouri River is a tributary of the Mississippi.
example_2: Dozens of tributaries feed the Amazon River.

definition: Existing in thought rather than in concrete form; not representational
etymology: Latin abstractus, from abstrahere (to draw away)
example_1: Mathematics deals with abstract concepts like infinity.
example_2: The abstract painting used only shapes and colors.

definition: A supreme ruler, especially a monarch; possessing supreme power
etymology: Old French soverain, from Latin superanus (above)
example_1: The sovereign greeted the visiting diplomat.
example_2: Each nation is sovereign within its own borders.

definition: To mark or adjust a scale; to measure precisely
example_1: Scientists calibrate instruments before taking measurements.
example_2: She calibrated her expectations before the difficult meeting.

definition: An unobstructed and wide view; a complete survey of a subject
example_1: From the summit, we had a panorama of the entire valley.
example_2: The documentary offered a panorama of modern life.

definition: A small piece of burning or glowing coal or wood in a dying fire
example_1: The last embers of the fire glowed red in the dark.
example_2: An ember from the campfire started a small blaze.

definition: The combination of elements to form a connected whole
etymology: Greek synthesis, from syntithenai (to put together)
example_1: Music is a synthesis of rhythm, melody, and harmony.
example_2: The essay's synthesis of ideas was impressive.

definition: The speed of something in a given direction
example_1: The rocket's velocity increased as it left the atmosphere.
example_2: The velocity of sound varies with temperature.

definition: A piece of thick textile with pictures woven in; a complex combination
example_1: The medieval tapestry depicted scenes of hunting.
example_2: Her life was a rich tapestry of experiences.

definition: Native and restricted to a certain place; regularly found in a particular area
example_1: The lemur is endemic to Madagascar.
example_2: Malaria is endemic in certain tropical regions.

definition: The path followed by a projectile or object; a course of development
etymology: Latin trajectoria, from traicere (to throw across)
example_1: The rocket followed a precise trajectory to the moon.
example_2: Her career trajectory took an unexpected turn.

definition: Able to be dissolved in liquid; able to be solved
example_1: Salt is soluble in water.
example_2: The problem proved soluble with the right approach.

definition: Open to more than one interpretation; unclear
example_1: The ambiguous message left everyone confused.
example_2: The law's language was deliberately ambiguous.

definition: A period of time marked by distinctive events; a division of geological time
example_1: The invention of the internet began a new epoch.
example_2: The Jurassic epoch lasted 56 million years.

definition: Able to withstand wear and last for a long time
example_1: The durable fabric lasted ten years of heavy use.
example_2: They sought a durable peace between the nations.

definition: A statement taken to be true as a basis for reasoning
example_1: It is an axiom that equals added to equals are equal.
example_2: The coach operated on the axiom that practice makes perfect.

definition: Calm, peaceful, and untroubled
example_1: The lake was serene at sunrise.
example_2: She remained serene despite the chaos around her.

definition: A glass shape that splits light into colors; a way of viewing something
etymology: Greek prisma (thing sawn), from prizein (to saw)
example_1: A prism splits sunlight into a rainbow.
example_2: Viewed through the prism of history, the decision made sense.

definition: Moving with great speed
example_1: The speedy cheetah was velocity personified.
example_2: A velocity of ideas flowed through her mind.

definition: Emitting light as a result of being heated; passionate or brilliant
example_1: The incandescent filament gave the bulb its glow.
example_2: Her incandescent performance won every award.

definition: Possessing supreme power; independent
etymology: Old French soverain, from Latin superanus
example_1: Each state is sovereign in domestic affairs.
example_2: She felt sovereign over her own destiny.

definition: The chemical breakdown of a substance by bacteria or yeast
example_1: Fermentation turns grape juice into wine.
example_2: Sourdough bread relies on natural fermentation.

definition: A chain or group of islands
example_1: The Greek archipelago draws millions of visitors.
example_2: Sailing through the archipelago took three weeks.

definition: Having tiny holes that allow liquid or gas to pass through
example_1: Porous rock absorbs rainwater slowly.
example_2: The border between the two territories was porous.

definition: To follow a winding course; to wander without direction
etymology: Greek Maiandros, a winding river in Turkey
example_1: The river meanders through the valley.
example_2: We meandered through the old city for hours.

definition: A deep fissure in the earth; a profound difference between people
example_1: A chasm split the glacier in two.
example_2: The chasm between rich and poor was growing.

definition: Active at night; relating to or done at night
example_1: Owls are nocturnal hunters.
example_2: She was a nocturnal person who did her best work after midnight.

definition: A change of form; a complete transformation
example_1: The caterpillar's metamorphosis into a butterfly takes weeks.
example_2: The city underwent a metamorphosis over two decades.

definition: Humor or lightness, especially in a serious situation
example_1: A touch of levity helped ease the tension.
example_2: He brought levity to the difficult meeting with a well-timed joke.

definition: A band of colors produced by light; a range of values or ideas
example_1: A rainbow displays the visible spectrum.
example_2: Her opinions span the full spectrum from conservative to radical.

definition: A long narrow crack or opening
example_1: The earthquake opened a fissure in the road.
example_2: A fissure developed in their friendship after the argument.

definition: Selfless concern for the well-being of others
example_1: Her altruism led her to donate anonymously for years.
example_2: True altruism asks nothing in return.

definition: A line that touches a curve at one point; a completely different topic
example_1: The line is tangent to the circle at that point.
example_2: He went off on a tangent about his childhood.

definition: Having or showing a feeling of vague longing or regret
example_1: She gave a wistful look at her old house.
example_2: His wistful smile suggested he was remembering happier times.

definition: A liquid able to dissolve other substances; able to pay all debts
example_1: Water is called the universal solvent.
example_2: After paying the loan, the company was finally solvent.

definition: A tube with mirrors and colored glass producing changing patterns; a complex shifting pattern
example_1: She peered through the kaleidoscope at the shifting patterns.
example_2: The festival was a kaleidoscope of music and color.

definition: More important than anything else; supreme
etymology: Old French paramont (above), from par (by) + amont (above)
example_1: Safety is paramount in any workplace.
example_2: The paramount concern was keeping the children safe.

definition: A gold coin formerly used in Britain
example_1: He paid for it with a golden sovereign.
example_2: The collection included sovereigns from three centuries.

definition: A whirling mass of water or air; a situation that draws in everything around it
example_1: The kayaker was swept into a vortex of white water.
example_2: She was pulled into a vortex of work and stress.

definition: Fluent or persuasive speaking or writing
etymology: Latin eloquentia, from eloqui (to speak out)
example_1: His eloquence moved even his opponents.
example_2: She won the debate through sheer eloquence.

definition: Either of two times when day and night are equal in length
example_1: The spring equinox marks the start of longer days.
example_2: Farmers plant around the equinox.

definition: Process plants use to convert sunlight into food
example_1: Photosynthesis produces the oxygen we breathe.
example_2: Without photosynthesis most life could not exist.

definition: Gradual wearing away of rock or soil by water or wind
etymology: Latin erosio, from erodere (to gnaw away)
example_1: Erosion carved the Grand Canyon over millions of years.
example_2: Cover crops prevent soil erosion.

definition: A vast flat treeless Arctic region with frozen subsoil
example_1: Reindeer graze across the vast tundra.
example_2: The tundra blooms with wildflowers in summer.

definition: To spend winter in a dormant inactive state
example_1: Bears hibernate through the coldest months.
example_2: Some computers hibernate to save energy.

definition: A seasonal wind system bringing heavy rainfall
example_1: The monsoon brings relief from summer heat.
example_2: Farmers depend on the monsoon for their crops.

definition: Relating to the sky or outer space
example_1: The celestial map showed every visible star.
example_2: She found celestial navigation fascinating.

definition: The ability to understand and share the feelings of others
example_1: Good doctors have empathy for their patients.
example_2: Teaching empathy starts at home.

definition: The quality of being honest and having strong moral principles
example_1: She was known for her integrity in all dealings.
example_2: Integrity means doing right when no one is watching.

definition: Persistence in doing something despite difficulty
example_1: Perseverance is the key to mastering any skill.
example_2: Her perseverance through hardship inspired everyone.

definition: A strong desire to know or learn something
example_1: Curiosity drove him to explore every library.
example_2: Curiosity is the engine of discovery.

definition: Sympathetic concern for the sufferings of others
etymology: Latin compassio, from com + pati (to suffer)
example_1: She showed great compassion for those in need.
example_2: Compassion without action is just sentiment.

definition: The quality of having experience and good judgment
example_1: Wisdom comes with experience not just age.
example_2: She had the wisdom to know when to stay silent.

definition: A willingness to take bold risks
example_1: It took audacity to challenge the established order.
example_2: The audacity of the plan surprised everyone.

definition: Careful and persistent work or effort
example_1: Diligence separates good students from great ones.
example_2: Her diligence in research produced breakthrough results.

definition: A story with a deeper hidden meaning
etymology: Greek allegoria, from allos (other) + agoreuein (to speak)
example_1: Animal Farm is an allegory of totalitarian government.
example_2: The painting was an allegory of justice.

definition: The art of arranging dance movements
example_1: The choreography for the ballet took months.
example_2: Brilliant choreography transformed the musical.

definition: A board for mixing paints; a range of colors used
example_1: She mixed colors on her palette.
example_2: The autumn palette of reds and golds was stunning.

definition: A spoken or written account of events
example_1: The film's narrative kept audiences guessing.
example_2: She shaped the narrative of her own life.

definition: A figure of speech comparing two unlike things
etymology: Greek metaphora, from meta + pherein (to carry)
example_1: Life is a journey is a common metaphor.
example_2: Her poetry used striking metaphors.

definition: A dark shadow or outline against a lighter background
etymology: French, named after Etienne de Silhouette
example_1: The silhouette of the mountain was dramatic at sunset.
example_2: She recognized him from his silhouette alone.

definition: A rhythmic sequence or flow of sounds
example_1: The poem's cadence was like music.
example_2: His speech had a steady reassuring cadence.

definition: A journey undertaken for a specific purpose
etymology: Latin expeditio, from expedire (to free the feet)
example_1: The Antarctic expedition lasted six months.
example_2: She joined an expedition to map the rainforest.

definition: To plan and direct the route of a ship or vehicle
example_1: She learned to navigate by the stars.
example_2: He navigated the complex bureaucracy skillfully.

definition: The science or practice of drawing maps
example_1: Cartography transformed exploration forever.
example_2: She studied cartography to understand trade routes.

definition: A long journey full of adventures
example_1: The road trip became a personal odyssey.
example_2: Getting the visa was an odyssey in paperwork.

definition: A stretch of land with its natural features
example_1: The rough terrain made hiking difficult.
example_2: She studied the terrain before planning her route.

definition: An instrument for finding directions; range or scope
example_1: The compass pointed steadily north.
example_2: Art is within the compass of every child.

definition: To undergo chemical breakdown by bacteria or yeast
example_1: Grapes ferment into wine over several weeks.
example_2: Discontent fermented into rebellion.

definition: Relating to cooking or the kitchen
example_1: She took a culinary tour of Italy.
example_2: His culinary skills impressed every guest.

definition: A set of rules or steps for solving a problem
example_1: The algorithm sorted thousands of records instantly.
example_2: She designed an algorithm to detect fraud.

definition: The repetition of a process to approach a result
example_1: Each iteration of the design improved on the last.
example_2: Software development relies on rapid iteration.

definition: Growing or decreasing at an ever-increasing rate
example_1: The population grew at an exponential rate.
example_2: Technology improves on an exponential curve.

definition: A quantity that can change; a symbol representing a value
example_1: In algebra x is the variable we solve for.
example_2: Temperature is the key variable in this experiment.

definition: A sentimental longing for the past
example_1: The old song filled her with nostalgia.
example_2: There's a nostalgia for simpler times.

definition: A feeling of pensive sadness with no obvious cause
example_1: The autumn rain brought a gentle melancholy.
example_2: His music expressed a beautiful melancholy.

definition: Great happiness and exhilaration
etymology: Latin elatio, from efferre (to carry out)
example_1: She felt pure elation crossing the finish line.
example_2: The team's victory produced waves of elation.

definition: A feeling of intense happiness and well-being
example_1: Winning the championship brought euphoria.
example_2: The medicine produced a temporary euphoria.

definition: A state of being pleasantly lost in thought
example_1: She sat in reverie watching the fire.
example_2: A reverie about childhood came over him.

definition: An overwhelming feeling of wonder or reverence
example_1: The mountain filled them with awe.
example_2: She stood in awe of the ancient cathedral.

definition: To travel across or through
example_1: They traversed the desert in three days.
example_2: The beam traverses the entire ceiling.

definition: To go up or climb
etymology: Latin ascendere, from ad + scandere (to climb)
example_1: The hikers ascended the rocky path.
example_2: She ascended through the ranks to CEO.

definition: To drive or push forward
etymology: Latin propellere, from pro + pellere (to drive)
example_1: Wind propelled the sailing ship.
example_2: Ambition propelled her to the top.

definition: To begin to move more quickly
example_1: The car accelerated onto the highway.
example_2: Climate change has accelerated in recent decades.

definition: To tend to meet at a point; to come together
etymology: Latin convergere, from com + vergere (to bend)
example_1: The roads converge at the old bridge.
example_2: Their ideas converged on the same solution.

definition: To fall or drop suddenly and quickly
example_1: The temperature plummeted overnight.
example_2: Stock prices plummeted after the news.

definition: Having the ability to speak fluently and coherently
etymology: Latin articulatus, from articulare (to divide into joints)
example_1: She gave an articulate response to every question.
example_2: An articulate speaker is a powerful force.

definition: The art of effective or persuasive speaking or writing
example_1: Political rhetoric often obscures the truth.
example_2: She mastered the rhetoric of persuasion.

definition: A form of a language spoken in a particular region
example_1: The dialect of the mountain villages was distinctive.
example_2: Linguists study dialect to trace migration patterns.

definition: The everyday language spoken by ordinary people
example_1: He wrote in the vernacular rather than formal Latin.
example_2: Tech vernacular can confuse outsiders.

definition: Living or occurring at the same time; modern
example_1: She was a contemporary of Shakespeare.
example_2: Contemporary art challenges traditional forms.

definition: The ancient past, especially before the Middle Ages
example_1: The ruins dated to classical antiquity.
example_2: Scholars of antiquity study Greece and Rome.

definition: Relating to the Middle Ages
example_1: Medieval knights wore heavy armor.
example_2: The medieval castle stood on the hilltop.

definition: A factual written account of events in order of time
example_1: The monk kept a detailed chronicle of daily life.
example_2: The documentary chronicles twenty years of change.

definition: Something handed down from the past; money left in a will
example_1: She left a legacy of kindness.
example_2: His scientific legacy still shapes the field.

definition: Basic physical structures needed for society to function
example_1: The country invested in infrastructure after the war.
example_2: Digital infrastructure includes cables and data centers.

definition: The lowest load-bearing part of a building; an underlying basis
example_1: The foundation was poured before the walls went up.
example_2: The discovery laid the foundation for modern chemistry.

definition: A supporting structure; a system underlying a concept
example_1: The framework supported the entire building.
example_2: We need a framework for evaluating proposals.

definition: Very complicated or detailed
example_1: The intricate lacework took months to complete.
example_2: The intricate plot kept readers guessing.

definition: To grasp mentally; to understand fully
etymology: Latin comprehendere, from com + prehendere (to grasp)
example_1: She could comprehend complex mathematical ideas.
example_2: The survey comprehends every household.

definition: A proposed explanation based on limited evidence
example_1: The scientist formed a hypothesis and tested it.
example_2: Her hypothesis proved correct after the experiment.

definition: To reach a conclusion by reasoning from evidence
etymology: Latin deducere, from de + ducere (to lead)
example_1: He deduced the answer from first principles.
example_2: Sherlock Holmes deduced everything from small clues.

definition: Based on observation or experience rather than theory
example_1: The empirical evidence supported the theory.
example_2: She preferred empirical data to speculation.

definition: To light up; to help clarify or explain
example_1: Candles illuminated the old chapel.
example_2: Her explanation illuminated a difficult concept.

definition: Regular movement back and forth between two positions
example_1: The oscillation of the pendulum keeps time.
example_2: Economic oscillations follow predictable patterns.

definition: Made up of exactly similar parts facing each other
example_1: The facade was perfectly symmetrical.
example_2: Nature creates symmetrical patterns in snowflakes.

definition: A person sent to represent others at a meeting
etymology: Latin delegare (to send on a commission)
example_1: Each country sent a delegate to the summit.
example_2: She delegated tasks to her capable team.

definition: General agreement among a group of people
example_1: The committee reached a consensus after debate.
example_2: Scientific consensus supports climate change.

definition: A person who publicly supports a cause or right
etymology: Latin advocatus, from advocare (to call to aid)
example_1: She was an advocate for children's education.
example_2: The advocate presented a compelling case.

definition: A system of government by the whole population
example_1: Democracy depends on an informed citizenry.
example_2: She studied the history of democracy.

definition: The desire to promote the welfare of others
example_1: His philanthropy funded libraries across the country.
example_2: She retired and devoted herself to philanthropy.

definition: To briefly immerse in boiling then cold water
example_1: Blanch the vegetables to preserve their color.
example_2: She blanched the almonds to remove their skins.

definition: To cook slowly in a small amount of liquid
example_1: Braise the short ribs for three hours.
example_2: Braising transforms tough cuts into tender delicacies.

definition: The outer peel of a citrus fruit used as flavoring; great enthusiasm
example_1: Add the zest of one lemon to the batter.
example_2: She attacked every project with great zest.

definition: To mix two liquids that do not normally combine
etymology: Latin emulsus, from emulgere (to milk out)
example_1: Whisk vigorously to emulsify the dressing.
example_2: Egg yolk helps emulsify mayonnaise.

definition: Relating to a system using only two values
example_1: Computers use binary code: ones and zeros.
example_2: The choice was not simply binary.

definition: The mathematical study of the properties of space
example_1: Topology explains why a donut and cup are the same shape.
example_2: Network topology describes how computers connect.

definition: Each way a set of things can be arranged in order
etymology: Latin permutatio, from permutare (to change completely)
example_1: There are 720 permutations of six items.
example_2: The permutations of the password were astronomical.

definition: A gradual change in a value over a distance
example_1: The color gradient shifted from blue to violet.
example_2: The steep gradient made cycling uphill exhausting.

definition: A river or stream flowing into a larger river
example_1: The Missouri River is a tributary of the Mississippi.
example_2: Dozens of tributaries feed the Amazon River.

definition: Native and restricted to a certain place
example_1: The lemur is endemic to Madagascar.
example_2: Malaria is endemic in certain tropical regions.

definition: Able to be dissolved in liquid
example_1: Salt is soluble in water.
example_2: The problem proved soluble with the right approach.

definition: Having tiny holes that allow liquid or gas to pass through
example_1: Porous rock absorbs rainwater slowly.
example_2: The border between territories was porous.

definition: To follow a winding course; to wander without direction
etymology: Greek Maiandros, a winding river in Turkey
example_1: The river meanders through the valley.
example_2: We meandered through the old city for hours.

definition: Active at night; relating to nighttime
example_1: Owls are nocturnal hunters.
example_2: She was a nocturnal person who worked best after midnight.

definition: A complete transformation of form or nature
example_1: A caterpillar undergoes metamorphosis into a butterfly.
example_2: The city underwent a metamorphosis over two decades.

definition: Humor or lightness, especially in a serious situation
example_1: A touch of levity helped ease the tension.
example_2: He brought levity to the difficult meeting.

definition: A band of colors produced by light; a range of values
example_1: A rainbow displays the visible spectrum.
example_2: Her opinions span the full political spectrum.

definition: A deep fissure in the earth; a profound difference between people
example_1: A chasm split the glacier in two.
example_2: The chasm between rich and poor was growing.

definition: Selfless concern for the well-being of others
example_1: Her altruism led her to donate anonymously for years.
example_2: True altruism asks nothing in return.

definition: Having a feeling of vague longing or regret
example_1: She gave a wistful look at her old house.
example_2: His wistful smile suggested happy memories.

definition: A tube producing changing patterns of color; a complex shifting pattern
example_1: She peered through the kaleidoscope at the shifting patterns.
example_2: The festival was a kaleidoscope of music and color.

definition: More important than anything else; supreme
example_1: Safety is paramount in any workplace.
example_2: The paramount concern was keeping the children safe.

definition: A whirling mass of water or air; a situation that draws in everything
example_1: The kayaker was swept into a vortex of white water.
example_2: She was pulled into a vortex of work and stress.

definition: A line that touches a curve at one point; a completely different topic
example_1: The line is tangent to the circle at that point.
example_2: He went off on a tangent about his childhood.

definition: A long narrow crack or opening
example_1: The earthquake opened a fissure in the road.
example_2: A fissure developed in their friendship.

definition: The process of coming together; the point where things meet
example_1: The convergence of the rivers formed a delta.
example_2: A convergence of events led to the discovery.

definition: Filled with lively energy and excitement
example_1: The children gave an exuberant welcome to the puppy.
example_2: Her exuberant personality filled every room.

definition: The highest point reached; the point directly above an observer
etymology: Medieval Latin cenit, from Arabic samt (path)
example_1: The sun reached its zenith at noon.
example_2: Her career was at its zenith when she won the award.

definition: Using very few words; brief and concise
example_1: His laconic reply was just two words.
example_2: She gave a laconic summary of the report.

definition: Sweet or musical; pleasant to hear
etymology: Latin mellifluus, from mel (honey) + fluere (to flow)
example_1: The singer had a mellifluous voice.
example_2: His mellifluous prose was a pleasure to read.

definition: Representing the most perfect example of something
example_1: She was the quintessential New Yorker.
example_2: The diner was quintessential American culture.

definition: Producing many works; abundant
etymology: Latin prolificus, from proles (offspring) + facere (to make)
example_1: Shakespeare was a prolific playwright.
example_2: The garden was prolific after the rain.

definition: Not transparent; not able to transmit light; hard to understand
example_1: The frosted glass was opaque.
example_2: Her motives remained opaque to everyone.

definition: Allowing light to pass through but not transparent
etymology: Latin translucens, from trans + lucere (to shine)
example_1: The translucent curtains diffused the sunlight.
example_2: Her translucent skin showed every vein.

definition: Liable to change rapidly; evaporating easily
example_1: Volatile markets made investing risky.
example_2: The volatile chemical evaporated quickly.

definition: To soak thoroughly; to fill to capacity
example_1: Saturate the soil before planting.
example_2: The market was saturated with similar products.

definition: To spread over a wide area; to make less concentrated
etymology: Latin diffundere, from dis + fundere (to pour)
example_1: Light diffused through the frosted glass.
example_2: She diffused the tension with a calm word.

definition: Movement of solvent molecules through a membrane; gradual absorption
example_1: Water passes through cell walls by osmosis.
example_2: She learned French by osmosis living in Paris.

definition: A substance that speeds up a chemical reaction; a cause of change
example_1: The catalyst reduced the reaction time significantly.
example_2: Her speech was a catalyst for the protest movement.

definition: To form crystals; to make or become definite and clear
example_1: Sugar crystallizes when the solution cools.
example_2: Her thoughts crystallized into a clear plan.

definition: In an inactive sleeping state; temporarily inactive
example_1: The dormant volcano last erupted 200 years ago.
example_2: The seeds remained dormant until spring.

definition: Green with lush vegetation
etymology: Old French verdant, from Latin viridis (green)
example_1: The verdant hills rolled down to the sea.
example_2: After the rain the garden was verdant and alive.

definition: Lasting for a long time; recurring regularly
example_1: The perennial river never ran dry.
example_2: Traffic is a perennial problem in the city.

definition: A layer of rock that holds water and allows it to flow
example_1: The aquifer supplied water to three cities.
example_2: Overuse depleted the underground aquifer.

definition: Matter that settles to the bottom of a liquid
example_1: Sediment built up on the riverbed over centuries.
example_2: The wine glass showed a fine sediment.

definition: To turn from liquid into vapor
example_1: The puddles evaporated in the afternoon sun.
example_2: Her enthusiasm quickly evaporated.

definition: A large naturally occurring community of plants and animals
example_1: The rainforest is the most biodiverse biome.
example_2: Climate change is altering every biome on Earth.

definition: To transfer pollen to fertilize plants
example_1: Bees pollinate most of our food crops.
example_2: Flowers attract insects to pollinate them.

definition: To move from one region to another seasonally
example_1: Monarch butterflies migrate thousands of miles.
example_2: Many families migrated west during the dust bowl.

definition: The natural home or environment of an animal or plant
example_1: The panda's habitat is the bamboo forest.
example_2: Deforestation destroys the habitat of countless species.

definition: A mutually beneficial relationship between different organisms
example_1: Clownfish and sea anemones live in symbiosis.
example_2: The partnership was a perfect business symbiosis.

definition: An animal that naturally preys on others
example_1: The wolf is an apex predator.
example_2: The startup was a predator in the market.

definition: The disguising of things to blend with surroundings
example_1: The leopard's spots are perfect camouflage.
example_2: She used humor as emotional camouflage.

definition: To breed or reproduce; to spread ideas
example_1: Plants propagate through seeds, spores, or cuttings.
example_2: Social media propagates information rapidly.

definition: A biological community of interacting organisms and their environment
example_1: The coral reef ecosystem is incredibly diverse.
example_2: The startup ecosystem of the city was thriving.

definition: Distance north or south of the equator; freedom of action
example_1: The city sits at 40 degrees north latitude.
example_2: She gave employees latitude to experiment freely.

definition: A circle of longitude passing through the poles
example_1: The prime meridian passes through Greenwich.
example_2: She reached the meridian of her career.

definition: A person who draws or produces maps
example_1: The cartographer spent years mapping the coastline.
example_2: Medieval cartographers placed sea monsters at edges.

definition: Supreme power or authority; self-governance
etymology: Old French soverain, from Latin superanus
example_1: The treaty recognized the sovereignty of both nations.
example_2: Personal sovereignty begins with knowing your values.

definition: To record events in the order they happened
example_1: She chronicled her travels in a series of essays.
example_2: The documentary chronicles twenty years of change.

definition: An object made by human hands, especially of historical interest
example_1: The museum displayed artifacts from ancient Egypt.
example_2: The software left artifacts in the code.

definition: A picture made of small colored pieces; a complex combination
etymology: Medieval Latin mosaicus, from Greek mouseios
example_1: The ancient mosaic depicted a Roman feast.
example_2: The city's culture is a mosaic of traditions.

definition: A short amusing or interesting story about a real event
example_1: He shared a funny anecdote about his first day.
example_2: The speech was full of memorable anecdotes.

definition: A simple story illustrating a moral lesson
example_1: The Good Samaritan is a famous parable.
example_2: The manager used a parable to explain teamwork.

definition: The use of humor or exaggeration to criticize something
example_1: The show uses satire to comment on politics.
example_2: Swift's writing was brilliant satire.

definition: A short saying expressing a traditional belief
example_1: A rolling stone gathers no moss is an old proverb.
example_2: She ended her speech with a wise proverb.

definition: A state of affairs the opposite of what one expects
example_1: It was an irony that the fire station burned down.
example_2: She appreciated the irony of the situation.

definition: A conclusion reached on the basis of evidence and reasoning
etymology: Latin inferentia, from inferre (to bring in)
example_1: The inference was reasonable given the available facts.
example_2: Good readers make inferences as they read.

definition: A comparison between two things to explain something
example_1: He used an analogy to explain the concept.
example_2: The analogy between brain and computer is imperfect.

definition: An opinion formed without proof; a guess
etymology: Latin conjectura, from conicere (to throw together)
example_1: It was pure conjecture without any evidence.
example_2: Scientists moved from conjecture to proven fact.

definition: To examine or inspect closely and thoroughly
example_1: She scrutinized the contract before signing.
example_2: Scientists scrutinize data before publishing results.

definition: To extract; to consider separately from material objects
etymology: Latin abstractus, from abstrahere (to draw away)
example_1: The mathematician abstracted the essential principle.
example_2: She abstracted the key points from the report.

definition: To become aware of through the senses; to understand
etymology: Latin percipere, from per + capere (to seize)
example_1: She could perceive a faint light in the distance.
example_2: He perceived the hidden meaning in her words.

definition: Expressing or involving prolonged thought
example_1: She was in a contemplative mood after the walk.
example_2: Monks live a quiet contemplative life.

definition: To express an idea clearly; to connect by a joint
example_1: He struggled to articulate his feelings.
example_2: She could articulate exactly what was wrong.

definition: Fluent or persuasive speaking or writing
etymology: Latin eloquentia, from eloqui (to speak out)
example_1: His eloquence moved even his opponents.
example_2: She won the debate through sheer eloquence.

definition: A subtle difference in meaning or expression
example_1: The translation lost important nuances of meaning.
example_2: She appreciated the nuance in his argument.

definition: Using more words than necessary; long-winded
example_1: His verbose emails took an hour to decode.
example_2: The verbose contract desperately needed editing.

definition: Expressed briefly and clearly
example_1: Her succinct explanation solved the problem instantly.
example_2: A succinct email is more likely to be read.

definition: Logically and consistently connected; able to speak clearly
etymology: Latin cohaerens, from cohaerere (to cling together)
example_1: She gave a coherent account of the events.
example_2: The argument was coherent and well-structured.

definition: Having mixed or contradictory feelings
example_1: She was ambivalent about leaving her hometown.
example_2: He felt ambivalent about the job offer.

definition: Dealing with things practically rather than theoretically
example_1: She took a pragmatic approach to the problem.
example_2: Pragmatic solutions are often more effective than ideal ones.

definition: Enduring pain or hardship without showing feelings
etymology: Greek stoikos, from Stoa Poikile (Painted Porch)
example_1: He was stoic in the face of terrible news.
example_2: Her stoic determination carried her through the ordeal.

definition: The capacity to recover quickly from difficulties
example_1: Her resilience after the setback was remarkable.
example_2: Building resilience prepares children for challenges.

definition: The quality of being determined; grip
example_1: Her tenacity in the face of rejection was inspiring.
example_2: The climber held on with remarkable tenacity.

definition: Courage and determination; coarse particles of stone
example_1: Grit and passion drive long-term achievement.
example_2: She had the grit to keep going when others quit.

definition: A modest view of one's own importance
example_1: True leadership requires humility.
example_2: She accepted the award with genuine humility.

definition: The quality of being thankful
example_1: She expressed deep gratitude for the help.
example_2: Practicing gratitude improves well-being.

definition: A state of happiness and satisfaction
example_1: Simple pleasures bring great contentment.
example_2: She found contentment in daily routines.

definition: A tender and persistent longing or desire
etymology: Old English gierning, from giernan (to desire)
example_1: She felt a deep yearning to return home.
example_2: His music was full of longing and yearning.

definition: A feeling of fear or anxiety about something
example_1: She entered the exam room with trepidation.
example_2: He faced the unknown with trepidation.

definition: Difficult to find, catch, or achieve
example_1: The solution remained elusive for years.
example_2: The elusive butterfly evaded every net.

definition: Involving or requiring strenuous effort
example_1: The arduous climb took six hours.
example_2: She completed the arduous task without complaint.

definition: Lasting for a very short time only
example_1: The ephemeral beauty of cherry blossoms lasts only days.
example_2: Social media fame can be ephemeral.

definition: Very deep; having great depth of knowledge or feeling
example_1: The book had a profound effect on her thinking.
example_2: She asked a profound question that silenced the room.

definition: So fine as to be hard to perceive; making clever distinctions
example_1: The subtle flavor improved with every sip.
example_2: Her subtle humor wasn't always noticed.

definition: Lacking interest or excitement; ordinary
example_1: She found beauty even in mundane moments.
example_2: The mundane task became surprisingly satisfying.

definition: Relating to deep feelings rather than intellect
example_1: The film created a visceral reaction.
example_2: Her visceral response to injustice drove her career.

definition: Characterized by subtle distinctions
example_1: His nuanced analysis impressed the panel.
example_2: She gave a nuanced performance full of depth.

definition: Based on random choice rather than reason
example_1: The rule seemed completely arbitrary.
example_2: An arbitrary decision frustrated the whole team.

definition: A statement that contradicts itself but may be true
example_1: The Ship of Theseus is a famous paradox.
example_2: It is a paradox that silence can be deafening.

definition: A statement taken as self-evidently true
example_1: It is an axiom that the whole is greater than any part.
example_2: The coach operated on the axiom that practice makes perfect.

definition: A division into two contrasted things
etymology: Greek dikhotomia, from dikho (in two) + temnein (to cut)
example_1: There is a false dichotomy between work and play.
example_2: The dichotomy between theory and practice was stark.

definition: Characterized by the belief that parts of something are connected
example_1: She took a holistic approach to wellness.
example_2: The holistic review considered every factor.

definition: To perform repeatedly; to develop through successive versions
example_1: Good designers iterate quickly and often.
example_2: She iterated on the recipe until it was perfect.

definition: To combine elements into a connected whole
example_1: She synthesized the research into a clear summary.
example_2: Chemists synthesize new compounds in the lab.

definition: To work jointly with others on an activity
etymology: Latin collaborare, from com + laborare (to work)
example_1: Artists and engineers collaborated on the project.
example_2: The teams collaborated across three time zones.

definition: To select and organize with care
example_1: She curated the exhibition over two years.
example_2: He curated a playlist for every mood.

definition: To make changes; to introduce new ideas
example_1: The company innovated constantly to stay ahead.
example_2: She innovated her teaching style every year.

definition: To adjust or mark a scale; to measure precisely
example_1: Scientists calibrate instruments before measurement.
example_2: She calibrated her expectations before the difficult meeting.

definition: To make less severe; to reduce the harmful effects
etymology: Latin mitigare, from mitis (gentle) + agere (to do)
example_1: Good planning can mitigate disaster.
example_2: She worked to mitigate the impact of the changes.

definition: To regard as probable; to look forward to
etymology: Latin anticipare, from ante + capere (to take)
example_1: She anticipated every possible problem.
example_2: He anticipated the question and had an answer ready.

definition: To make an action easier; to help bring about
example_1: The workshop facilitated open dialogue.
example_2: Her calm demeanor facilitated agreement.

definition: To spread something widely
example_1: The report was disseminated across all departments.
example_2: Social media disseminates information instantly.
